title: The Wildmenu
subtitle: 'wildmenu', 'wildmode', 'wildoptions' — making {key:Tab} at the {key::} prompt actually pleasant.
summary: Three options shape Tab completion at the : prompt. 'wildmenu' turns on the visual menu; 'wildmode' picks the cycling strategy; 'wildoptions' tweaks behavior. Together they turn : into a tolerable file picker.

blocks.text: By default, vintage Vim's Tab completion at the {key::} prompt just *replaces* what you typed with the first match, with no preview of the alternatives. {key:Tab} again cycles. It works, but you're guessing. Three options turn this into a real menu.

rows: Turns on the visual completion menu — a list of candidates above the {key::} line that you can walk through with {key:Tab}/{key:Shift-Tab} (or {key:Ctrl-N}/{key:Ctrl-P}).
How Tab behaves: complete to longest common prefix first, cycle, list-only, or some combination.
Extra wildmenu features. The most useful value is `pum` (Neovim 0.9+): show completions as a *popup menu* over the buffer rather than a single-line strip.

blocks.text: These four lines in your config buy you a competent file picker without any plugin:

```
set wildmenu
set wildmode=longest:full,full
set wildoptions+=pum
set wildignorecase
```

Read it as: show the menu, on the first Tab complete to the longest unambiguous prefix and open the menu, on the second Tab cycle through entries; render the menu as a popup (Neovim only); be case-insensitive for filename matching.
Suppose your cwd contains `README.md`, `render_html.py`, `render_markdown.py`, and `requirements.txt`. With the recipe above:

rows: Command-line becomes `:e re`; the menu lists every matching file: `README.md`, `render_html.py`, `render_markdown.py`, `requirements.txt`.
Command-line becomes `:e README.md`; first menu entry highlighted.
Type `n` to extend the prefix to `ren`, then Tab again — menu narrows to just the two `render_*` files.

blocks.text: {key:Shift-Tab} walks backward through the menu — useful when you've blown past the entry you wanted. {key:Ctrl-E} cancels the menu and restores what you originally typed.

blocks.title: Why `wildmode=longest:full,full` is the sweet spot
blocks.text:
`'wildmode'` is a comma-separated list of *stages*. The first Tab uses stage 1, the second uses stage 2, the third uses stage 1 again, and so on. Each stage is one of:

  • `full` — cycle through matches.
  • `longest` — complete to the longest common prefix.
  • `list` — show all matches without completing.
  • `lastused` — for buffers, order by recency.
  • `:full` (colon-suffix on `longest`) — also open the menu after completing.

`longest:full,full` means: first Tab completes the common prefix *and* opens the menu (so you can both see your options and avoid retyping shared characters); subsequent Tabs cycle. This is the strict superset of every other reasonable choice — you never lose information, and you never have to press more keys than the alternative settings would have required.

`'wildoptions'+=pum` (Neovim only) replaces the cramped one-line strip with a true popup, the same UI Vim uses for Insert-mode completion. `'wildignorecase'` makes `:e Rea<Tab>` and `:e rea<Tab>` behave the same — useful on case-insensitive filesystems and case-sensitive ones both.

blocks.title: What `:set wildmenu` actually replaces
blocks.text:
Without `'wildmenu'`, Tab in the {key::} prompt is a *one-shot completion*: Vim picks a match, substitutes it for what you typed, and waits for your next keystroke. You have to press {key:Ctrl-D} separately if you want to see alternatives, and the listing is printed below the command line as static text.

With `'wildmenu'`, the same Tab interaction becomes *stateful*: Vim opens an in-place menu, tracks the highlighted candidate, and lets {key:Tab}/{key:Shift-Tab} (or {key:Ctrl-N}/{key:Ctrl-P}) walk it. The completion logic underneath is identical — same context inference (`:e` → files, `:set` → options, `:help` → tags) — but the *presentation* shifts from "guess and re-tab" to "see and choose." Once you've used it for a week, vanilla Tab completion feels unfinished.
